title: RNNs, LSTMs & GRUs (Historical Context)
summary: Sequential models that process one token at a time, maintaining a hidden state. LSTMs/GRUs add gating to control information flow. Transformers replaced them because: (1) RNNs are inherently sequential (no parallelism), (2) long-range dependencies are hard despite gates, (3) attention provides direct access to any position. Understanding why they failed informs transformer design choices.
key_insights: RNN: h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t) — hidden state carries all history. Sequential by construction.
Vanishing gradient: gradients through long chains of h_t multiplications shrink to zero
LSTM: adds cell state c_t with forget/input/output gates — controls what to remember/forget
GRU: simplified LSTM with 2 gates instead of 3 — similar performance, fewer parameters
Why transformers won: parallel training (no sequential dependency), direct long-range access (attention), scalability (more compute = better results)
RNNs still useful for: streaming/online processing, very long sequences where O(n^2) attention is prohibitive

mathematical_core.rnn: h_t = tanh(W_hh * h_{t-1} + W_xh * x_t + b)
mathematical_core.lstm_forget: f_t = sigma(W_f * [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f) — what to forget from cell state
mathematical_core.lstm_input: i_t = sigma(W_i * [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i) — what new info to store
mathematical_core.lstm_cell: c_t = f_t * c_{t-1} + i_t * tanh(W_c * [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c)
mathematical_core.lstm_output: h_t = o_t * tanh(c_t) — what to output from cell state

enables: Understanding why transformers were designed the way they are
related: TRANSFORMER.SELF_ATTENTION — attention solves the long-range dependency problem RNNs couldn't
TRANSFORMER.POSITIONAL_ENCODING — needed because transformers gave up sequential processing
TRANSFORMER.MASKING — simulates sequential processing in parallel

notes: Historical context — understand what problem transformers solved. Not a primary learning target for architecture innovation.
